title: Adaptive Prototype Granularity in Frozen-Feature Contrastive Adaptation
description: This paper proposes a controlled empirical study of whether adaptive per-class prototype counts help in frozen-feature contrastive adaptation. The contribution is intentionally incremental: under a fixed frozen CLIP backbone and matched lightweight heads, it tests whether conservative split/merge selection on cosine-normalized embeddings improves over class-level contrastive adapters, fixed-K prototype heads, and a spread-preserving frozen-feature comparator.
motivation: Several nearby papers already cover most ingredients in isolation: Supervised Contrastive Learning provides the base objective, Perfectly Balanced argues that latent subclass structure matters, Contrastive Adapters studies frozen-feature robustness adaptation, SBCL studies subclass-aware end-to-end training, CoPA studies prototype adaptation near pretrained transfer, and hyperspherical or vMF classifier papers already justify spherical prototype modeling. What remains genuinely worth measuring is the narrower practical question of whether adaptive prototype granularity itself is useful in a frozen-feature adaptation regime once backbone, optimizer, head width, and compute are all controlled.
proposed_approach: Freeze CLIP ViT-B/16, cache features once, and train only lightweight adapter heads. Compare class-level contrastive adaptation, fixed-K prototype heads, and an adaptive prototype head that shares the same cross-entropy, supervised contrastive, prototype-alignment, and occupancy losses. The adaptive method uses a conservative held-out penalized von Mises-Fisher split/merge criterion on normalized features with occupancy floors, small dataset-specific prototype caps, and a threshold-free Waterbirds fallback based on bootstrap agreement; the proposal also pre-registers a simple sensitivity analysis with two stricter fallback margins so the main claim does not depend on a bespoke validation rule.

related_work.title: Supervised Contrastive Learning
related_work.how_it_differs: Provides the base contrastive loss but does not model within-class multimodality or adapt prototype count.

related_work.title: The Power of Contrast for Feature Learning: A Theoretical Analysis
related_work.how_it_differs: Motivates the problem by showing that contrastive objectives can discard transfer-relevant information, but it does not offer a frozen-feature subclass-selection method.

related_work.title: Perfectly Balanced: Improving Transfer and Robustness of Supervised Contrastive Learning
related_work.how_it_differs: The closest conceptual predecessor because it argues latent subclasses matter beyond class spread, but it studies end-to-end supervised contrastive learning rather than frozen-feature head adaptation. The proposed work only compares against a PB-inspired frozen-feature spread comparator.

related_work.title: Contrastive Adapters for Foundation Model Group Robustness
related_work.how_it_differs: The main direct baseline because it uses frozen CLIP features and lightweight adaptation for robustness, but it does not ask whether adaptive same-class prototype granularity helps beyond class-level contrastive supervision.

related_work.title: Subclass-balancing Contrastive Learning for Long-tailed Recognition
related_work.how_it_differs: A close methodological neighbor because it uses subclass-aware learning, but it is designed for end-to-end long-tailed recognition rather than a frozen-backbone model-selection study.

related_work.title: Mind the Gap Between Prototypes and Images in Cross-domain Finetuning
related_work.how_it_differs: A close adjacent prototype-adaptation paper in pretrained transfer, but not a controlled frozen-CLIP head-only comparison of fixed versus adaptive per-class prototype counts.

related_work.title: Hyperspherical Prototype Networks
related_work.how_it_differs: Shows that hyperspherical prototype classifiers are already established, so spherical prototypes are not novel here; it does not study frozen-feature robustness adaptation or adaptive prototype count selection.

related_work.title: Towards Calibrated Hyper-Sphere Representation via Distribution Overlap Coefficient for Long-tailed Learning
related_work.how_it_differs: Represents vMF or hyperspherical classifier literature that motivates sphere-aware scoring, but not adaptive subclass model selection in frozen CLIP features.

related_work.title: Learning Robust Classifiers with Self-Guided Spurious Correlation Mitigation
related_work.how_it_differs: A recent robustness baseline family that addresses spurious correlations rather than same-class prototype granularity.

related_work.title: Learning Transferable Visual Models From Natural Language Supervision
related_work.how_it_differs: Supplies the frozen CLIP backbone and unit-sphere feature geometry used in the study rather than the adaptive head mechanism under test.

hypothesis: If frozen CLIP features retain recoverable within-class multimodality relevant to subgroup robustness or fine-grained recognition, then conservative adaptive per-class prototype selection should outperform both class-level contrastive adapters and matched fixed-K prototype heads under the same head capacity and training budget. If those gains do not appear or are unstable under simple fallback sensitivity checks, the correct conclusion is that adaptive prototype granularity adds little beyond existing frozen-feature adapters in this regime.

confirm: Across 3 seeds, the adaptive method improves Waterbirds worst-group accuracy over both the class-level contrastive adapter and the fixed-K=2 contrastive prototype baseline by roughly 1.5 absolute points or more without a material drop in average accuracy.
Across the same 3-seed protocol, the adaptive method improves at least one primary CUB metric over the best fixed-K baseline.
A nontrivial minority of classes retain K_c > 1 with occupancies above the pre-registered floors across repeated runs, indicating the adaptive mechanism is active rather than degenerate.
Waterbirds conclusions remain qualitatively unchanged when the threshold-free bootstrap fallback is replaced by two pre-registered stricter margins.
refute: A fixed-K prototype head or the class-level contrastive adapter matches or beats the adaptive method within normal variation on both datasets.
The non-contrastive prototype baseline explains the gains, implying adaptive selection is not the key factor.
The PB-inspired spread-preserving comparator closes the gap completely, suggesting spread preservation is sufficient.
Selected prototype counts or Waterbirds gains are unstable across seeds or disappear under the simple fallback sensitivity analysis.
